interpretation: Measures short-term liquidity. Below 1.0 signals potential cash flow problems. Too high may indicate idle assets.
Acid test excluding inventory. More conservative than current ratio. Critical for inventory-heavy businesses.
Most conservative liquidity measure. SaaS typically has high cash reserves.
Absolute dollar measure of operational cushion. Negative working capital is common in restaurants (fast inventory turns).
Core profitability before overhead. Restaurant food cost target ~30-35%. SaaS should be >70%.
Bottom line profitability. SaaS may be negative during growth phase. Consulting typically highest.
Profitability from core operations excluding interest and taxes. Key metric for operational efficiency.
Cash flow proxy. Useful for comparing companies with different depreciation and capital structures.
Restaurant-specific. Fine dining 30-35%; fast casual 25-30%; QSR 28-32%.
Restaurant-specific. Bar-focused 18-20%; full-service restaurant 20-24%.
Restaurants: front-of-house + back-of-house combined. Consulting: primary cost driver.
Restaurant-specific. Most critical restaurant metric. Over 65% = trouble.
Productivity measure. SaaS highest due to scalability. Restaurants lowest due to labor intensity.

formula: (Current Period Revenue - Prior Period Revenue) / Prior Period Revenue
interpretation: Growth rate varies widely by stage. Early SaaS >100%; mature SaaS 20-40%.
SaaS-specific. Net new MRR including expansion minus churn.
Varies enormously by business model. Must compare to LTV.
SaaS gold standard. Below 3:1 = unsustainable acquisition. Above 5:1 = underinvesting in growth.

formula: CAC / Monthly Gross Profit per Customer
interpretation: SaaS-specific. How long to recover acquisition cost. Over 18 months is concerning.

formula: End Period MRR from Prior Cohort / Start Period MRR
interpretation: SaaS-specific. >100% means expansion exceeds churn. Best-in-class >120%.
SaaS-specific. Monthly churn. >5% monthly = existential threat.
Leverage measure. Construction and restaurants often higher due to capital needs.
What percentage of assets are financed by debt. Lower = less risk.
Ability to pay interest. Below 1.5 = high risk. SaaS often has minimal debt.
Bank covenant standard. Below 1.25 often triggers covenant default.
How quickly AR is collected. Restaurants mostly cash/card. Construction AR often slow.
Average collection period in days. Construction/consulting clients often pay slowly.
How quickly the company pays its vendors.
Average payment period. Stretching too far damages vendor relationships.
Restaurants should turn inventory weekly. Retail varies by product type.
How many days inventory sits before being sold.
Days between paying for inventory and receiving cash. Negative = customer funds operations.
How efficiently fixed assets generate revenue. SaaS is asset-light.
Overall efficiency of asset utilization.
How effectively assets generate profit.
Return to owners. Highly leveraged companies may show artificially high ROE.
True return on all capital deployed. Best measure of value creation.
SaaS-specific. Balances growth vs profitability. Top quartile SaaS >40%.
SaaS/startup-specific. Monthly cash consumption rate.
SaaS/startup-specific. Months of cash remaining. Below 6 months = urgent fundraise.

formula: Net New ARR / Prior Quarter S&M Spend
interpretation: SaaS-specific. Sales efficiency. >1.0 = efficient growth. <0.5 = spending too much.
Real estate burden. Restaurants and retail are location-dependent.
SaaS invests heavily in growth marketing. Construction relies on relationships.
SaaS-specific. Investment in product. Below 15% risks stagnation.
Administrative overhead. SaaS higher in early stage; should decline with scale.
Construction-specific. Future work pipeline. Below 1.0 = need new contracts.
Project-based businesses. Track per-project to identify underperformers.
Consulting-specific. Below 65% = overstaffed or poor sales pipeline.
SaaS-specific. Track MoM for pricing power and upsell effectiveness.
Revenue level where profit = 0. Critical for startups and seasonal businesses.
How much operating income changes relative to revenue change. Higher = more sensitive to revenue changes.

formula: (New MRR + Expansion MRR) / (Churned MRR + Contraction MRR)
interpretation: SaaS-specific. Growth efficiency. Measures ability to grow despite churn.
Actual tax burden. S-Corps and partnerships pass through to owners. Watch for state variations.
Reasonableness test for S-Corp officer compensation. IRS scrutinizes if too low.
